page101_figure_0.png: This technical diagram, titled **"Figure 5.0-1 Product Realization"**, illustrates a sequential process for product realization, structured into two main hierarchical levels: high-level phases and detailed sub-processes. **Overall Structure:** The diagram presents a left-to-right flow, indicating a progression through different stages. While there are no explicit arrows connecting these lower-level boxes to the upper-level phases, their vertical alignment and horizontal progression imply their relationship to the broader phases. 1. **Product Implementation:** * This process is positioned below and to the left, generally corresponding to the **DESIGN REALIZATION** phase. * It has an orange header and lists three bullet points: * Acquire * Make/Code * Reuse 2. **Product Integration:** * This process is positioned to the right of "Product Implementation," also generally corresponding to the **DESIGN REALIZATION** phase. * It has an orange header and lists two bullet points: * Assembly * Functional Evaluation 3. **Product Verification:** * This process is positioned to the right of "Product Integration," generally corresponding to the **EVALUATION PROCESSES** phase. * It has an orange header and lists three bullet points: * Functional * Environmental * Operational Testing in Integration & Test Environment 4. **Product Validation:** * This process is positioned to the right of "Product Verification," also generally corresponding to the **EVALUATION PROCESSES** phase. * It has an orange header and lists one bullet point: * Operational Testing in Mission Environment 5. **Product Transition:** * This process is positioned to the right of "Product Validation," generally corresponding to the **PRODUCT TRANSITION PROCESS** phase. * It has an orange header and lists two bullet points: * Delivery to Next Higher Level in PBS * Delivery to Operational System **Relationships and Flow:** The diagram illustrates a clear sequential flow from design through evaluation to final product transition. page124_figure_4.png: This technical diagram, titled **"Figure 5.3-2 Example of End-to-End Data Flow for a Scientific Satellite Mission"**, illustrates the complete data flow for a scientific satellite mission, from planning and command generation to data capture and analysis, including external stimuli. The accompanying text states: "End-to-end testing is an integral part of the verification and validation of the total (mission)". The diagram is structured into four main vertical sections, representing different systems or domains, and two main horizontal processes. **Main System/Domain Divisions (Vertical Axes):** 1. **EXTERNAL SYSTEMS** (leftmost column) 2. **GROUND SYSTEM** (second column from left) 3. **FLIGHT SYSTEM** (third column from left) 4. **EXTERNAL STIMULI** (rightmost column) **Main Process Divisions (Horizontal Processes):** * **Uplink Process** (indicated above the top row of nodes in the Ground System) * **Downlink Process** (indicated below the bottom row of nodes in the Ground System) All process steps are represented by gray-filled oval nodes, and relationships are shown by blue arrows indicating the direction of flow. --- **Detailed Description of Nodes, Arrows, and Relationships:** **I. EXTERNAL SYSTEMS Column:** * **Mission Planning** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Mission Planning" to "Planning" in the Ground System. * A bidirectional arrow connects "Mission Planning" and "Scientific Community". * **Scientific Community** (oval node): * A bidirectional arrow connects "Scientific Community" and "Mission Planning". * An arrow points from "Archival" in the Ground System to "Scientific Community". **II. GROUND SYSTEM Column:** This column is divided into Uplink and Downlink processes. **A. Uplink Process:** * **Planning** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Mission Planning" (External Systems) to "Planning". * An arrow points from "Planning" to "Command Generation". * An arrow points from "Planning" to "Software Loads". * An arrow points from "Analysis" (Ground System, Downlink Process) back to "Planning", indicating a feedback loop. * **Command Generation** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Planning" to "Command Generation". * An arrow points from "Command Generation" to "Transmission". * **Software Loads** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Planning" to "Software Loads". * An arrow points from "Software Loads" to "Transmission". * **Transmission** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Command Generation" to "Transmission". * An arrow points from "Software Loads" to "Transmission". * An arrow points from "Transmission" to "Spacecraft Execution" in the Flight System. **B. Downlink Process:** * **Data Capture** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" (Flight System) to "Data Capture". * An arrow points from "Data Capture" to "Analysis". * **Analysis** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Data Capture" to "Analysis". * An arrow points from "Analysis" to "Archival". * An arrow points from "Analysis" back to "Planning" (Uplink Process), indicating a feedback loop. * **Archival** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Analysis" to "Archival". * An arrow points from "Archival" to "Scientific Community" (External Systems). **III. FLIGHT SYSTEM Column:** * **Spacecraft Execution** (oval node): This is a central node in the Flight System. * An arrow points from "Transmission" (Ground System) to "Spacecraft Execution". * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" to "Instrument Set A". * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" to "Instrument Set B". * An arrow points from "Instrument Set A" to "Spacecraft Execution". * An arrow points from "Instrument Set B" to "Spacecraft Execution". * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" to "Data Capture" (Ground System). * **Instrument Set A** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" to "Instrument Set A". * An arrow points from "Instrument Set A" to "Spacecraft Execution". * Arrows point from "X-rays" and "Visible" (External Stimuli) to "Instrument Set A". * **Instrument Set B** (oval node): * An arrow points from "Spacecraft Execution" to "Instrument Set B". * An arrow points from "Instrument Set B" to "Spacecraft Execution". * Arrows point from "Ultraviolet", "Infrared", and "Microwave" (External Stimuli) to "Instrument Set B". **IV. EXTERNAL STIMULI Column:** * **X-rays** (oval node): An arrow points from "X-rays" to "Instrument Set A" (Flight System). * **Visible** (oval node): An arrow points from "Visible" to "Instrument Set A" (Flight System). * **Ultraviolet** (oval node): An arrow points from "Ultraviolet" to "Instrument Set B" (Flight System). * **Infrared** (oval node): An arrow points from "Infrared" to "Instrument Set B" (Flight System). * **Microwave** (oval node): An arrow points from "Microwave" to "Instrument Set B" (Flight System). --- **Summary of Relationships and Flow:** The diagram illustrates a cyclical process. The **Uplink Process** begins with "Mission Planning" and "Scientific Community" in External Systems, leading to "Planning" in the Ground System. From "Planning", commands and software loads are generated and transmitted to the "Spacecraft Execution" in the Flight System. In the **Flight System**, "Spacecraft Execution" interacts with "Instrument Set A" and "Instrument Set B". These instrument sets receive various "External Stimuli" (X-rays, Visible, Ultraviolet, Infrared, Microwave). The **Downlink Process** starts with "Spacecraft Execution" sending data to "Data Capture" in the Ground System. This data undergoes "Analysis" and is then "Archived". The "Archival" data is then made available to the "Scientific Community". Crucially, "Analysis" provides feedback to "Planning" in the Ground System, allowing for iterative refinement of mission operations. The "Scientific Community" also interacts with "Mission Planning", closing the overall loop.

page137_figure_6.png: This technical diagram, titled **"Figure 5.5-1 Product Transition Process"**, illustrates a sequential process for transitioning a product, showing inputs, process steps, and outputs, along with their relationships to other external processes. The diagram is structured as a flowchart with a central vertical sequence of six main process steps (represented by yellow rectangles), inputs on the left (light blue shapes), and outputs on the right (light blue shapes). **Main Process Steps (Yellow Rectangles, from top to bottom):** 1. **Prepare to Conduct Product Transition:** The initial step in the process. 2. **Evaluate the End Product, Personnel, and Enabling Product Readiness for Product Transition:** This step assesses the preparedness of the product, the team, and supporting products for the transition. 3. **Prepare the End Product for Transition:** This involves readying the product itself for handover. 4. **Transition the End Product to the Customer with Required Documentation Based on the Type of Transition Required:** This is the core handover step, tailored to the specific transition type. 5. **Prepare Sites, as Required, Where the End Product Will Be Stored, Assembled, Integrated, Installed, Used, and/or Maintained:** This step focuses on the physical or operational environment for the product. 6. **Capture Work Products from Product Transition Activities:** The final step involves documenting and collecting artifacts from the transition process. **Inputs to the Process (Light Blue Shapes on the Left):** * **End Product to Be Transitioned:** This input feeds into the first step, "Prepare to Conduct Product Transition." * **Documentation to Accompany the Delivered End Product:** This input is explicitly labeled as coming "From Technical Data Management Process" and feeds into the third step, "Prepare the End Product for Transition." * **Product Transition Enabling Products:** This input feeds into the fifth step, "Prepare Sites, as Required..." **Outputs from the Process (Light Blue Shapes on the Right):** * **Delivered End Product with Applicable Documentation:** This output originates from the first two process steps ("Prepare to Conduct Product Transition" and "Evaluate the End Product..."). It is directed "To End User or Product Integration Process (Recursive Loop)," indicating that the delivered product might feed back into further integration or user processes. * **Product Transition Work Products:** This output originates from the fourth process step ("Transition the End Product to the Customer..."). It is directed "To Technical Data Management Process." * **Realized Enabling Products:** This output originates from the final process step ("Capture Work Products from Product Transition Activities"). It is directed "To Product Implementation, Integration, Verification, Validation, and Transition Processes." **Arrows and Relationships:** * **Vertical Downward Arrows:** Indicate the sequential flow of steps within the main "Product Transition Process." Each step must be completed before proceeding to the next. * **Horizontal Rightward Arrows (from Inputs to Process):** Show external entities or products feeding into specific stages of the transition process. * **Horizontal Rightward Arrows (from Process to Outputs):** Show the results or artifacts generated by the transition process, which are then directed to other external processes or stakeholders. **Hierarchical Structures, Lifecycle Stages, or Numbered Phases:** * The diagram does not show explicit numbered phases or a hierarchical structure within the main process steps; they are presented as a linear sequence. * It represents a specific **lifecycle stage** (Product Transition) within a broader product lifecycle, as evidenced by the inputs coming "From Technical Data Management Process" and outputs going "To End User or Product Integration Process" and "To Product Implementation, Integration, Verification, Validation, and Transition Processes." * The "Recursive Loop" mentioned for the "Delivered End Product" suggests an iterative or feedback relationship with the "End User or Product Integration Process." In summary, the diagram precisely outlines the steps, inputs, and outputs involved in a product transition, emphasizing the flow of activities and the interaction with related management and implementation processes.
